auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: The carve-out is standard (parties do not intend to cap the payment obligation itself), but its interaction with late fees, fee increases, and the absence of dispute mechanics creates a hazard. If Lattice can unilaterally increase fees at renewal (uncapped per Section 3.5), demand payment of the increase as 'amounts owed under the Order Form,' and impose compounding late fees on any delayed payment, Customer's total exposure is uncapped and the liability cap in Section 8.6 provides no protection. The absence of invoice-dispute withholding (per coverage_pass and finding commercial-terms-analyst-004) means Customer must pay to avoid late fees, even if the underlying fee increase or charge is disputed. On high-leverage deals where Customer has limited negotiating power post-signature, this creates vendor pricing leverage that extends indefinitely.
auditor_findings.position: Clarify the carve-out: 'amounts owed under any Order Form' applies only to undisputed fees for Subscription Services and Professional Services ordered in writing by Customer and not subject to good-faith dispute. Fees arising from unilateral vendor adjustments, alleged overages, or usage disputes are subject to the liability cap until resolved. Alternatively, cap renewal fee increases (CPI or 5%) to eliminate uncapped exposure on the carve-out.
auditor_findings.fallback: Clarify that late fees on disputed amounts do not accrue until the dispute is resolved, and that any fees disputed in writing within 15 business days are not 'owed' for purposes of the carve-out until the dispute is resolved in Lattice's favor.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 3.2: 'All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: The accepted finding commercial-terms-analyst-003 proposes capping renewal fee increases at CPI or 5% with 90-day notice. However, Section 3.2 still contains Net-60 payment terms. If the renewal-increase cap is accepted but payment terms remain at Net-60, Customer receives only 30 days of actual decision time between fee-increase notice (90 days before renewal) and the non-renewal deadline (60 days before renewal, per accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-001 proposing reduction to 30 days). The coherence issue: the 90-day fee-increase notice in the proposed edit to Section 3.5 assumes Customer has adequate time to evaluate and decide whether to renew at the new price, but the 60-day payment terms in Section 3.2 create cash-flow planning friction that may compress the effective evaluation window. More critically, the suspension-for-nonpayment provision in Section 3.4 (30 days overdue triggers suspension after 10-day notice) interacts badly with Net-60 terms: if Customer routes invoices through a standard Net-30 AP process during the evaluation period, any invoice issued in the 60-day window before renewal becomes overdue exactly when Customer is deciding whether to renew, creating suspension risk that forces a rushed renewal decision. This is a cross-section coherence trap that will not be visible to the counterparty reviewer unless explicitly called out.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: Accepted finding commercial-terms-analyst-003 addresses renewal fee increases in isolation. Accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-003 addresses the Net-60 + suspension interaction. But neither addresses the temporal coherence issue: a 90-day fee-increase notice (proposed in commercial-terms-analyst-003) gives Customer three decision windows, but Net-60 payment terms mean any invoice issued in the 60-day pre-renewal window is still outstanding when the non-renewal deadline arrives, forcing Customer to decide on renewal while an invoice that may reflect the OLD pricing is still in AP. If the proposed 30-day non-renewal window (termination-remedies-analyst-001) is accepted, Customer receives fee-increase notice at T-90 days, must decide by T-30 days, but is simultaneously managing an invoice due at T-0 (60 days from issuance at T-60). The friction is operational, not legal, but it materially increases the risk of inadvertent renewal because finance and procurement are managing overlapping deadlines with incomplete information about whether the new fees have been accepted.
coherence_findings.position: If the 90-day fee-increase notice (proposed in commercial-terms-analyst-003) and 30-day non-renewal window (proposed in termination-remedies-analyst-001) are both accepted, reduce payment terms to Net-30 to eliminate the invoice-overlap period, OR extend fee-increase notice to 120 days so Customer has 90 days of clear decision time before the non-renewal deadline.
coherence_findings.fallback: Retain Net-60 payment terms but add explicit language in Section 3.5 stating that any fee increase notice triggers a one-time right for Customer to request Net-30 payment terms for invoices issued in the 60-day period preceding the renewal date.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.2: 'LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED, ERROR-FREE, OR FREE OF HARMFUL COMPONENTS, OR THAT CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-001 proposes adding a data-breach super-cap at 3x annual fees or available cyber insurance limits, with explicit carve-out for breaches of Section 5 (Customer Data; Data Security; Privacy) involving unauthorized access or disclosure of Customer Data. However, Section 8.2's warranty disclaimer explicitly states that Lattice does not warrant that Customer Data will be secure or not lost or damaged. As drafted, the disclaimer in Section 8.2 arguably negates any warranty of data security, which could allow Lattice to argue that a data breach does not constitute a 'breach' triggering the super-cap in the proposed edit to Section 8.6, because Lattice never warranted data security in the first place. This creates internal incoherence: the proposed super-cap presumes that unauthorized access to Customer Data constitutes a breach of Section 5, but Section 8.2 disclaims any warranty that would make such access a breach. The counterparty reviewer may not catch this unless it is explicitly flagged.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The proposed super-cap in risk-allocation-analyst-001 is predicated on the assumption that Lattice's data-security obligations in Section 5.2 are enforceable performance covenants, not merely aspirational. However, Section 8.2's broad disclaimer ('CUSTOMER DATA WILL BE SECURE OR NOT OTHERWISE LOST OR DAMAGED') could be read to disclaim any warranty of security, reducing Section 5.2 to a best-efforts obligation with no breach remedy. If Lattice's counsel raises this issue in redline negotiation, Customer will have to either (a) remove the disclaimer in Section 8.2 as it relates to Section 5.2 obligations, or (b) rework the super-cap language to clarify that breaches of the affirmative covenants in Section 5.2 (not warranties) trigger enhanced liability. The current state creates ambiguity that could be exploited to defeat the super-cap.
coherence_findings.position: Narrow the warranty disclaimer in Section 8.2 to exclude data-security obligations: 'LATTICE DOES NOT WARRANT THAT THE SUBSCRIPTION SERVICES WILL BE UNINTERRUPTED OR ERROR-FREE, EXCEPT THAT LATTICE WILL MAINTAIN THE INFORMATION SECURITY PROGRAM DESCRIBED IN SECTION 5.2.' OR clarify in the proposed super-cap language (risk-allocation-analyst-001) that the super-cap applies to 'a Party's breach of the affirmative obligations set forth in Section 5' rather than 'a Party's breach of Section 5,' to make clear that the super-cap attaches to the covenant, not a warranty.
coherence_findings.fallback: Add savings language to the proposed super-cap: 'For the avoidance of doubt, nothing in Section 8.2 limits either Party's liability for breach of the affirmative obligations set forth in Section 5.2 and 5.3.'
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: United States (Delaware law per Section 10.1); Delaware enforces warranty disclaimers and may read broad disclaimers to negate implied covenants unless the covenant is separately and clearly articulated.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 2.3: 'Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement' + Section 8.6 liability cap
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes removing the 'sole and exclusive remedy' language from Section 2.3 so that Customer retains termination rights under Section 4.3 for material breach including repeated SLA failures. However, Section 2.3 also states that 'Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement.' If the 'sole remedy' language is removed as proposed, and Customer terminates for repeated SLA failures under Section 4.3, the question arises: does Customer forfeit any accrued service credits upon termination, or do service credits remain payable in addition to termination? The contract does not address this. More importantly, if service credits are NOT liquidated damages and do NOT limit Lattice's rights, does that mean Lattice can separately pursue Customer for amounts owed under the Order Form even after issuing service credits? The language cuts both ways. The proposed edit to Section 2.3 eliminates sole-remedy but does not address the 'do not limit rights' clause, creating ambiguity about whether service credits are additive to or in lieu of other remedies post-termination.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The 'do not limit Lattice's rights' language in Section 2.3 was likely drafted to preserve Lattice's right to terminate or suspend for non-payment even if service credits are owed. However, once the 'sole remedy' framing is removed (per performance-obligations-analyst-001), the 'do not limit rights' language creates a reciprocal question for Customer: if Customer terminates for SLA failures under Section 4.3 (after the sole-remedy language is removed), does Customer retain the right to collect accrued service credits, or does termination moot those credits? The contract is silent. In a high-value SaaS deal, accrued service credits could be material (potentially months of subscription fees if SLA failures are severe), and the ambiguity over whether they survive termination could lead to a post-termination dispute. The proposed edit should clarify that service credits accrued prior to termination remain payable to Customer even if Customer terminates for cause.
coherence_findings.position: If the 'sole and exclusive remedy' language is removed from Section 2.3 per performance-obligations-analyst-001, add clarifying language: 'Service credits accrued prior to any termination of this Agreement shall remain payable to Customer. For the avoidance of doubt, the availability of service credits does not limit either Party's rights to terminate this Agreement for material breach in accordance with Section 4.3.'
coherence_findings.fallback: Retain the 'do not limit Lattice's rights' language but add reciprocal language: 'Service credits do not limit either Party's rights under this Agreement, including rights to terminate for cause, and service credits accrued prior to termination remain payable.'
